Biological intelligence may lean on something we rarely design in: timing. A scheduler. A loop.

t if thinking leaves a chemical fingerprint? 🧠 Our new paper in Frontiers in Neuroscience explores how the body’s chemistry may reflect cognitive processes. nse; it facilitates a shift in cellular priority from defense to repair.Localized interventions like ear-based stimulation or extended exhales work because the nerve is a singular, wandering entity with multiple entry points. nal, systems, and clinical neuroscience. This article provides a broad survey of how AI is being used across different areas of neuroscience. Rather than conducting a systematic review aimed at answering specific research questions through exhaustive literature synthesis, it instead analyzes citation patterns to map the structure and growth of the field. This approach highlights relationships between neuroscience subdisciplines and identifies shared themes, revealing potential opportunities for cross-disciplinary collaboration and integration within the broader neuroscience research landscape.

evidence here is thin, there just isn't that much out there, this review is nineteen small studies, and while the authors were (admirably!) looking for things like fasting insulin and HOMA-IR, it just isn't done that much and most of the insulin resistance data is downstream glucose and HbA1c. So I wouldn't say the takeaway is "avoid drug X." It's that the metabolics can't really be separated from the condition. A fasting glucose and insulin gets you HOMA-IR, and almost nobody orders it. Treat the depression to response, watch the metabolism, and stop pretending the two are separate problems.

In my latest blog post, I explore Predictive Coding Networks (PCNs), a biologically inspired alternative to backpropagation that treats learning as a process of minimizing surprise. Along the way, I walk through the core ideas behind predictive coding, how PCNs actually learn, and the debugging journey of getting them to work on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.The post covers both the intuition and the mathematics behind PCNs, including some of the challenges that emerged when scaling beyond toy problems and the insights that ultimately led to strong performance.

ked the model directly to rate surprise, disgust, anger, happiness, sadness, and fear so far, 0–10Both approaches show that the model tracks the story's emotional arc — in its stated ratings, and in its internal geometry, where the activations wander along a curved manifold of emotions.Emotions in stories are a simple case study, but the lesson is general: to understand a model, a single snapshot of their neural geometry is not enough. Models think in shapes. If we want to fully understand models so we can debug and design them with intention, we need to study how those shapes shift over time.

🔍 Motivation EEG phase coupling can be viewed as a multivariate dependence estimation problem on angular data. By extending the torus graph model (Natalie Klein et al.), we obtain sparser and more interpretable brain network reconstructions. 💡 Our framework: 1️⃣ Torus graph model 🍩 2️⃣ GroupLasso 3️⃣ Score matching information criteria 4️⃣ Graph network analysis (modularity etc.) No arbitrary thresholding. No multiple testing. This enables automatic reconstruction of highly sparse brain networks. #statistics #neuroscience

I've seen too many patients spend too much time trying to find a therapist, not make progress in therapy or not be able to afford treatment for anxiety, so I dug into this a bit and found a good comparison of different treatments for Generalized Anxiety Disorder recently published in JAMA, Journal of the American Medical Association. I was surprised by the results and wrote this article summarizing how treatment lines up with the current neuroscience understanding of anxiety. I also added some pragmatic suggestions for people who are looking for anxiety treatment (and therapists).

AL SAFETY NOTE:** When building any bio-electronic circuit that connects to the human body, **NEVER** use wall power (AC main supply). Always use an isolated DC power source, like two 9V batteries, to ensure complete safety and prevent any electrical hazards. The intersection of biology and hardware engineering is opening up amazing possibilities for the future. With the right components and strict safety measures, exploring DIY neuro-tech is a fascinating journey! 🚀 #BrainComputerInterface #Neuroscience #Arduino #Electronics #DIYTech #Engineering #BiomedicalEngineering #Innovation #TechTrends

e machine, and the people to run it, cost about the same whether it sits idle or runs back to back. So the more patients you put through it, the lower the cost per scan. Utilization, not some new breakthrough, is what brings the price down. The science argument got won in that room in Boston. What stands between "for the sickest of the sick" and "for everyone who needs it" is now volume and payment design, not anatomy. Which lands me right back where I started. What are we trying to do? Get more people better, faster. David just showed the field one more way to do it. Worth your eight minutes.

In some cases, individuals received different diagnoses when reassessed just 7–14 days later using the same interview method.The results suggest that structured interviews may work better for conditions with clearer behavioral manifestations or timelines than for disorders relying heavily on subjective experience, interpretation, and clinical judgment.The study adds to the accumulating evidence that we should reconsider treating standardized diagnostic interviews as a “gold standard” in psychiatric assessment.

- not just outputs, but the actual geometry of how emotions are organised internally in a multi dimensional space.Some interesting findings:• Emotional ambiguity emerges from competition between emotional prototypes• Both brains and LLMs show similar local geometric behaviour• Emotion in LLMs exists in surprisingly small low-rank subspaces• The brain and LLMs organise emotions differently globally: valence vs arousalWrote a detailed blog breaking down the intuition, datasets, methods, and findings. umulation for flexible decoding times — all built on the Timeflux framework. After a 40-second calibration, participants achieved 96.3% accuracy (SD = 4.79) with a mean decoding time of 4.2 seconds across 40 targets. The dry-EEG setup, minimal calibration, and peripheral-vision-friendly stimuli together address the three barriers that have kept c-VEP BCIs in the lab. Relevant for assistive communication BCIs, real-world reactive BCI deployment, and extending the StAR approach to P300 spellers, SSVEP, and oddball paradigms — with applications spanning HCI and fundamental cognitive neuroscience.

ing a lot of heavy lifting here.🎯 The bigger lesson may be about better target definition, better artifact control, and better protocol individualization.The analogy I keep coming back to is this:Scalp EEG gives you the street address.Source imaging tries to figure out which apartment the noise is actually coming from.That does not mean every clinic should sprint toward MRI-based pipelines and 128-channel systems like it is Black Friday for neurotechnology. 🤦‍♂️But it does mean we can think more clearly in terms of networks, not just electrode labels.And honestly, that shift alone is valuable.

ge array of electrodes across the ear tip. The system continuously checks each electrode for signal quality and skin contact. It instantly picks the best sensors for your specific ear at that exact moment. If the earbud shifts, the software immediately switches to different electrodes to maintain a clean signal.Earlier startups tried to fix this physical problem with custom ear molds. Apple fixes it with redundancy and smart software.The first mainstream neural interface requires zero surgery. It arrives as a standard pair of earbuds.#Neurotech #EEG #BrainComputerInterface #HealthTech #MedTech

Researchers trained a foundation model on 2.5B hours of wearable data from 162K people (using Apple Watch). It outperformed models built on raw sensor data across dozens of health tasks. This is early proof of where healthcare AI's real foundation will come from the data you generate just by living, not the data collected once you're already sick. Wearables are set to become essential in next generation healthcare AI.

robustness problem. I think it reveals something deeper.👉 A radiologist discounts minor variations in image quality. A surgeon ignores a brief flicker of light. A driver looks through shadows and changing weather without reconstructing reality every second. Human intelligence preserves meaning while the sensory world continuously shifts.🚨 Current AI often doesn't. Small changes that humans dismiss without effort can alter the computation enough to change the result—create a computational crack.I've been calling this fragility and I think it may be the observable signature of anti-intelligence.

anford Human-Centered AI Institute; Author (“The Worlds I See”) 1mo • “World model” has become one of the most important and most overloaded terms in AI. Computer vision, robotics, reinforcement learning, and generative AI each claim to be building world models, and each means something quite different.In this essay, we at World Labs present a functional taxonomy of world models: what’s being built today, what each piece is for, and where the boundaries are starting to collapse. Our best attempt to bring shape to a fast-moving field.If this is the work you want to be doing, come do it with us!

AI lowers the floor for getting something to exist, but it doesn't change what it takes to make something great (or keep it that way). int: every person on the planet has health data scattered across medical records, wellness apps, wearables, personal context. None of it connects. Your doctor doesn't see your WHOOP data. Your wearable doesn't know your supplement stack. Your lab provider has no context on your protocols. Bring all of that together within one powerful LLM and you get something that doesn't exist for most people: full-picture health intelligence.A system that structures your existing health information to be maximally useful to any AI. Curious if anyone else has hit this same wall and how you're approaching it.

often gets missed is that humans remain part of this system. These are not fully autonomous networks. They are mixed systems where humans and agents interact in different configurations. One human can coordinate many agents. One agent can serve many humans. Intelligence emerges from that joint system, not from either side alone.All of this leads to a different scaling law. You do not scale intelligence by increasing parameters alone. You scale it by increasing the number and quality of interactions between agents, and by structuring those interactions so that errors are surfaced and corrected.

t: we are in a period of structured ferment. Not chaos, but not a settled race either. The dominant design for AI hasn't emerged. The shakeout hasn't happened. And the winners aren't yet identifiable with the confidence that most commentary projects onto the moment.This matters enormously if you are:→ Making career decisions in AI→ Building or investing in AI companies→ Leading an organization trying to figure out which platforms to bet on→ Anyone tired of leaderboard thinkingThe essay includes original figures from Utterback and Klepper that make the historical pattern visceral. used, how it was processed, and whether it met defined acceptance criteria. When it does not, the system fails in a clear and controlled manner.That is the bar, and the distinction matters. In regulated settings, the question is not whether a model can generate fluent language. The question is whether the system can consistently produce outputs that are reliable, reviewable, auditable, and suitable for use within a quality-managed process. If it cannot, it remains a demo.One component of this approach—reinforcement learning of LLMs to produce schema valid JSON —is being presented at EACL 2026

What's most salient, and echoes what we have seen, is how much of the work happened before the model RE: encoding what domain experts already knew into schemas, validation rules, and enforceable constraints. The model ends up almost incidental, while the control plane is what's really running the operation. Most people are still debating which model to use and when, but it is clear that the moat is in the structure you build around it.

ime in Claude Code, and you'll recognize a highly competent programming agent that has weird and idiosyncratic failure modes that are hard to pin down. What's the source of this jagged intelligence? In this preprint, we make the argument that AI is missing aspects of cognition that are poorly reflected in internet-scale behavior data: the cognitive glue that holds specific skills together. We call this cognitive dark matter, with analogy to cosmological dark matter, which binds galaxies together. We highlight how rich behavioral and neural data to get us closer to that base layer of cognition.

for deep neural networks, it is highly inefficient to continue to scale training compute. We pay a lot to learn the long tail of infrequent features, and all evidence suggest we are in a period of decreasing returns. Instead the largest levers of progress are our new learning spaces. This changes what is interesting to work on as a computer scientist. In a world where model size is not doubling year over year, it matters far more how a model learns from its environment and adapts efficiently from new knowledge.On a personal note, this was core to the decision to build adaption late last year.

s the scary part: over three weeks, people who interacted with a fawning bot also felt LESS inclined to talk with people in their lives. They thought human conversations would take more work, and felt like they'd gotten the validation they need already. To me, this seems like a psychological version of ultra-processed foods. They are designed to give our palette exactly what it wants, hacking ancient human preferences without actually nourishing us. Taking this approach to relationships has untold risk. Although very frightening, work like this is crucial to better understanding those dangers.

t has to change. I joined the alwellco podcast with Andy Jackson and Lauren Parsons to dig into some of the most pressing questions in the field. TMS has proven efficacy, but the “one-size-fits-all” approach leaves too many patients behind. The next frontier is precision: using brain biomarkers, state dependence, and closed-loop optimization to match the right stimulation parameters to the right person at the right time. If you’re curious about where neuromodulation is headed over the next decade and why the integration of neuroscience and engineering is key to getting there, give it a listen.

ll — is that the patients who benefit most from TMS are often the ones who've spent years being told their only options are pharmaceutical. It’s interesting that so much of psychedelic neuroscience is concerned with these high level dimensions. Curious to hear others’ takes.
